abstraction-with-base-interfaces.label: Abstraction With Base Interfaces
abstraction-with-base-interfaces.summary: Treat a base class like a promise: every child class must provide the method that helpers expect.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Define a shared catalog interface
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: In `models/catalog_item.py`, define `CatalogItem.label()` so it raises `NotImplementedError`. Keep `Book.label()` as the concrete implementation, then run `main.py` to print `Book: Dune`.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: The base method raises `NotImplementedError`; the Book subclass returns its formatted label.

help.concept: An abstract-style base method defines the behavior subclasses must provide.
help.hint_1: The base method raises `NotImplementedError`; the Book subclass returns its formatted label.
help.hint_2: Check the named file and compare your code with each requirement in the prompt.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.starterCode:
# Run this provided demo after completing the model methods.
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Dune")
print(book.label())

main_py.content:
# Run this provided demo after completing the model methods.
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Dune")
print(book.label())

models_book_py.content:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

class Book(CatalogItem):
    # TODO: return Book: <title>.
    pass

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Dune")
print(book.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Dune")
print(book.label())

0.message: Define CatalogItem in the base model file.

1.message: Keep CatalogItem constructible with a title.

3.message: Print the Book label from main.py.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Add Movie to the same interface
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: Implement `Movie.label()` in `models/movie.py` so it returns `Movie: <title>`. Keep Book on the same base interface. `main.py` should print `Book: Dune` and `Movie: Moana`.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: Movie should inherit from CatalogItem and override label() just like Book.

help.concept: Different subclasses satisfy the same base contract with their own output.
help.hint_1: Movie should inherit from CatalogItem and override label() just like Book.
help.hint_2: Check the named file and compare your code with each requirement in the prompt.

for item in items:

for item in items:

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Use a helper that trusts the interface
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: Complete `show_labels(items)` in `services/catalog_view.py`. Return one string with each item's `label()` on its own line. `main.py` is already wired to print the returned string.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: Join the results of `item.label()` with newline characters.

help.concept: The helper depends only on the shared interface and does not need type checks.
help.hint_1: Join the results of `item.label()` with newline characters.
help.hint_2: Check the named file and compare your code with each requirement in the prompt.

from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
print(show_labels(items))

main_py.content:

from models.movie import Movie

print(show_labels(items))

from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
print(show_labels(items))

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
print(show_labels(items))

services_catalog_view_py.content:
def show_labels(items):
    return "\n".join(item.label() for item in items)

0.message: Return both labels so main.py prints two lines.

sc-interface-purpose.title: Why define the interface in CatalogItem?
sc-interface-purpose.prompt: Why is a base interface useful in this module?
sc-interface-purpose.hint: Think about what later code can rely on.

help.concept: A base interface tells the rest of the program which method every subclass should provide.
help.hint_1: The answer is about a shared contract.
help.hint_2: It is not about forcing every subclass to return identical text.

options.a: It tells later code that every catalog item should provide `label()`.
options.b: It prevents subclasses from changing behavior.
options.c: It removes the need for subclasses entirely.
options.d: It makes `Book` and `Movie` the same class.

fb-interface-method-name.title: Complete the shared interface method
fb-interface-method-name.prompt: Fill in the expected method name for the interface.
fb-interface-method-name.hint: Use the same method name from this module's helper examples.

help.concept: The base interface and the subclasses agree on one shared method name.

dr-interface-build-order.title: Order the interface steps
dr-interface-build-order.hint: Describe the contract first, then fulfill it, then use it.

help.concept: The interface comes before the subclasses and helpers that depend on it.
help.hint_1: The base class describes the expected method first.
help.hint_2: The helper uses the finished interface last.

tokens.t1: Write `label()` in CatalogItem as the expected interface

sc-interface-helper-focus.title: Choose what the helper should care about
sc-interface-helper-focus.prompt: When `show_labels(items)` loops through mixed objects, what should it focus on most?
sc-interface-helper-focus.hint: Pick the shared contract, not the exact subclass.

help.concept: Good interface-based code depends on shared behavior instead of subclass-specific branches.
help.hint_1: The helper should not care whether the object is a Book or Movie.
help.hint_2: It only needs the shared method to exist.

options.a: The order the subclasses were written in the file
options.b: The exact subclass name for every object
options.d: How many times the constructor was called

sketch-0.title: An interface is a promise the children agree to keep
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
An interface is like a school form that says, "Every club must provide a contact name."

The clubs can be different, but they all agree to provide the same kind of answer.

In code, a base class can describe that promise:

```python
class NotificationChannel:
    def message(self):
        raise NotImplementedError("Subclasses must define message")
```

This says: every child class should have a `message()` method. The base class names the promise; the child classes fulfill it.

sketch-1.title: Each child fulfills the promise in its own way

sketch-2.title: Helpers can trust the promise
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
A helper can work with any object that keeps the promise:

```python
def collect_messages(channels):
    messages = []
    for channel in channels:
        messages.append(channel.message())
    return messages
```

The helper does not need to know whether the channel is email or SMS. It only trusts the interface: each object has `message()`.

That is why interfaces help larger programs stay flexible.
